cited_text:
There's a kind of excited curiosity that's both the engine and the
rudder of great work. It will not only drive you, but if you let
it have its way, will also show you what to work on.
response_text: The engine/rudder framing is precise in a way most motivation advice isn't. It does two jobs at once — it drives you forward and it points the direction. Most people treat these as separate problems.

cited_text:
Knowledge expands fractally, and from a
distance its edges look smooth, but once you learn enough to get
close to one, they turn out to be full of gaps.
response_text: This is the best one-sentence explanation of why expertise feels discouraging at first and then accelerating. The smooth-from-a-distance illusion is what puts beginners off and keeps pretenders comfortable.

cited_text:
Per-project
procrastination is far the more dangerous. You put off starting
that ambitious project from year to year because the time isn't
quite right. When you're procrastinating in units of years, you can
get a lot not done.
response_text: The brutality of "get a lot not done" lands. The reason this form of procrastination is so dangerous is the next point: it disguises itself as productivity. You're always busy — just never on the thing that matters most.

cited_text:
[[8](#f8n)]  One reason per-project procrastination is so dangerous is that it
usually camouflages itself as work. You're not just sitting around
doing nothing; you're working industriously on something else. So
per-project procrastination doesn't set off the alarms that per-day
procrastination does. You're too busy to notice it.
response_text: This is the version of the trap most ambitious people fall into. The alarm never goes off because surface-level you look productive. It's only visible in hindsight — or when you ask the question directly.

cited_text:
The trouble with planning is that it only works for achievements
you can describe in advance. You can win a gold medal or get rich
by deciding to as a child and then tenaciously pursuing that goal,
but you can't discover natural selection that way.
response_text: The gold medal vs. natural selection contrast is the sharpest version of this argument I've seen. Planning optimises for known targets. The best work is usually in unknown territory — so plans are a ceiling, not a floor.

cited_text:
Boldly chase outlier ideas, even if other people aren't interested
in them — in fact, especially if they aren't. If you're excited
about some possibility that everyone else ignores, and you have
enough expertise to say precisely what they're all overlooking,
that's as good a bet as you'll find.
response_text: The qualifier "enough expertise to say precisely" is doing real work here. Without it this is just contrarianism. With it, it's a genuine edge. The combination of conviction + precision is rare — most people have one or neither.

cited_text:
Originality isn't a process, but a habit of mind. Original thinkers
throw off new ideas about whatever they focus on, like an angle
grinder throwing off sparks. They can't help it.
response_text: This reframes "how do I be more creative" as the wrong question entirely. You don't switch originality on for a session — you either have the underlying habit or you're building it. The angle grinder image is good: it's a **property of the material**, not the motion.

cited_text:
Indeed, if you think about it, a good new idea has to seem bad to
most people, or someone would have already explored it. So what
you're looking for is ideas that seem crazy, but the right kind of
crazy.
response_text: The follow-on distinction between exciting-bad and merely-bad ideas is the practical payoff. Exciting-bad ideas are "rich in implications." Merely-bad ones are "depressing." That's a real heuristic — the emotional texture of a bad idea tells you something about whether it's the right kind of bad.

cited_text:
But ideas that are the right kind of
crazy tend to be exciting; they're rich in implications; whereas
ideas that are merely bad tend to be depressing.
response_text: The follow-on distinction between exciting-bad and merely-bad ideas is the practical payoff. Exciting-bad ideas are "rich in implications." Merely-bad ones are "depressing." That's a real heuristic — the emotional texture of a bad idea tells you something about whether it's the right kind of bad.

cited_text:
Doing great work is a depth-first search whose root node is the
desire to. So "If at first you don't succeed, try, try again" isn't
quite right. It should be: If at first you don't succeed, either
try again, or backtrack and then try again.
response_text: The CS framing earns its place here — it separates backtracking (useful) from abandoning the root (fatal). The distinction between giving up on an approach vs. giving up on the goal is one most people don't make explicitly. They either never backtrack or they backtrack all the way out.

cited_text:
People show much more originality in solving problems than in
deciding which problems to solve. Even the smartest can be surprisingly
conservative when deciding what to work on. People who'd never dream
of being fashionable in any other way get sucked into working on
fashionable problems.
response_text: The asymmetry between problem selection and problem solving is underappreciated. Smart people apply all their creativity *within* the box, then accept the box as given. Loom is partly a bet that better inputs shift which problems feel visible.

cited_text:
Curiosity is the key to all four steps in doing great work: it will
choose the field for you, get you to the frontier, cause you to
notice the gaps in it, and drive you to explore them. The whole
process is a kind of dance with curiosity.
response_text: This is the essay's thesis restated as a checklist. Every step has the same root cause, which means if curiosity is compromised — by prestige-chasing, fashion, fear — the whole pipeline degrades. It's not one input among many. It's the load-bearing variable.
